title: Digoxin Therapy and Toxicity
subtitle: The nurse manages digoxin — checking the apical pulse for a full minute, holding for a rate under 60, watching potassium and a narrow therapeutic level, and recognizing toxicity (nausea, visual halos, bradycardia).

patient.name: Digoxin
patient.admitDate: The nurse safely administers and monitors digoxin.

nursesNote: The nurse safely administers digoxin, a cardiac glycoside that slows the heart rate and strengthens contraction, used in heart failure and atrial fibrillation. Before each dose the nurse counts the apical pulse for one full minute and holds the dose, then notifies the provider, if an adult's rate is below 60 (or below 70 in a child). Digoxin has a narrow therapeutic range (about 0.5-2 ng/mL), so levels are monitored. Low potassium increases the risk of toxicity, so the nurse watches the potassium level, especially in patients also taking potassium-wasting diuretics. Early signs of toxicity are gastrointestinal, including anorexia, nausea, and vomiting; visual changes such as blurred vision and yellow-green halos are classic; and cardiac effects include bradycardia and dysrhythmias. If toxicity is suspected, the nurse holds the drug, checks the digoxin and potassium levels, monitors the heart rhythm, and notifies the provider. Severe toxicity is treated with digoxin immune Fab. The nurse teaches the patient to check the pulse, report nausea or visual halos, maintain potassium-rich intake as advised, and not double up on missed doses.
providerOrders: Count the apical pulse for one full minute before each digoxin dose
Hold digoxin and notify the provider if the adult pulse is under 60
Monitor the digoxin level and the serum potassium
Assess for toxicity: nausea, visual halos, and bradycardia
Keep digoxin immune Fab available for severe toxicity

opts.text: Digoxin slows the heart rate and strengthens contraction
The apical pulse is counted for one full minute first
Hold the dose if an adult's pulse is below 60
Digoxin has a wide, forgiving therapeutic range
Low potassium increases digoxin toxicity
Yellow-green halos can signal toxicity
A therapeutic level is about 0.5-2 ng/mL
Potassium-wasting diuretics raise toxicity risk
Anorexia and nausea can be early toxic signs
A high potassium level always causes toxicity
Bradycardia and dysrhythmias are serious effects
Digoxin immune Fab treats severe toxicity

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order giving a scheduled digoxin dose

opts.text: Count the apical pulse for one full minute
Review the latest dig level and potassium
Give the dose if the pulse is 60 or above
Assess for nausea or visual changes
Document the pulse, dose, and findings

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Give the dose or Hold and notify?

opts.text: Adult apical pulse is 78 and regular
Adult apical pulse is 52
Patient reports nausea and yellow halos
Dig level and potassium are within range
Serum potassium is low at 2.8
Stable vital signs and no symptoms

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient on digoxin has nausea, halos, and HR 48
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Hold the digoxin dose
Check the digoxin level and potassium
Monitor the heart rate and rhythm
Notify the provider of the findings
Give digoxin immune Fab if ordered
Counting the apical pulse for a full minute first
Giving the dose when the adult pulse is 50
Reporting nausea and yellow-green halos
Ignoring a low potassium level
Teaching the patient to take their own pulse
Doubling up after a missed digoxin dose
Keeping digoxin immune Fab available for severe toxicity
Skipping the digoxin level because the pulse is fine
